entry.title: Material Standard Cost Estimate (CO-PC-PCP)
entry.content:
Material cost estimation is a process of calculating the expected cost of manufacturing or procuring material. This is used to determine the standard cost of a product based on its components, production activities, and the overhead costs.

Cost estimation considers several factors such as raw material price, labor costs, machine costs, overhead costs, and subcontracting costs. It ensures accurate product costing, inventory valuation, and profitability analysis. This will be used to determine the pricing of the product and budgeting.

Integration with other module
Integration with other module

Costing materials with a quantity structure enables you to calculate the cost of goods manufactured and cost of goods sold for each product unit without reference to orders. A quantity structure typically consists of a bill of material (BOM) and a routing. But in the process industries, a recipe is used instead of a BOM. You can use the results of material cost estimates with a quantity structure to value materials at standard cost.

The principle of product cost estimation
The principle of product cost estimation

We can cost out individual materials, or multiple materials simultaneously in costing runs. Then, standard costs can be updated to the material master as the future standard price or current standard price.

A. Costing Structure
1. Costing Variant (T-Code: OKKN)
You can define how the cost estimate is calculated by customizing the cost control which contains costing type, valuation variant, and date control.


Maintain costing variant

  • Costing Type: Determines the purpose of the cost estimate (e.g., standard cost estimate, simulation, product cost collector, etc).
  • Valuation Variant: Determines whether standard price or moving average price is used. It also specifies how materials, activity types, and overheads are valued.
  • Quantity structure (BOM & Routing): BOM is to define raw materials and semi-finished products used as the component to produce a product. Routing is to define the operations, work centers, and activities for production. 

    B. Steps in Material Cost Estimate with Quantity Structure
    These are the end-to-end process flow from creating material to material price analysis. But in this article we will just focus on the material cost estimate calculation part.


    Material cost estimation flow in SAP

    1. Go to transaction code CK11N

    Here you need to fill the material and plant. For the costing data, choose the costing variant you want to use as you’ve been configuring it in T-Code OKKN before.


    Material cost estimate with quantity structure initial screen

    The dates need to be in the period of the cost center you maintain when maintaining the activity price at T-Code KP26.


    Material cost estimate with quantity structure initial screen (Dates)

    2. Make sure all cost structure error status is green. Usually there will be an error message if the calculation is not complete. For example you didn’t fill the cost center planning in KP26. Or you enter the wrong period of pricing so the cost will not be calculated. 

    On the cost component view, you can see the COGS and COGM has been calculated based on production routing, bill of material, and other expenses.


    Material cost estimate with quantity structure

    3. After you get the cost estimate, go to transaction code CK24 to mark and release the standard price. Insert the posting period and make sure the quantity that cost estimate updated successfully is not 0.


    Mark standard price

    Then, you can check the price at material master (T-Code: MM02/MM03 → Costing 2).
    The marked price will appear as the future price, but you can also still see the current price before we run the material cost estimate.


    Material master: Costing 2 Future Price

    If you are sure with the standard price calculation, you can go back to CK24 to release the standard price.


    Release standard price

    You can check back on the material master, the material cost estimate is moved to the current price after we release it.


    Material master: Costing 2 Current Price

     

    So that's basically how you calculate the standard cost from material cost estimates. Feel free to discuss more if you have any other insights or questions! Hope this helps to understand the basics of material cost estimation. 

     

    A lifetime learner,
    Dafina

entry.title: An in-depth look at clean core business processes, read our Clean Core for S/4 HANA Cloud paper!
entry.content:
Have you ever wondered where to start to understand more about the benefits of a clean core for your enterprise system and how to achieve one? If you are already on a clean core journey do you want tips and tricks to get more value out of it?

Then our new paper on Clean Core business processes for SAP S/4HANA Cloud is for you! It brings you in-depth insights into the importance of standardization of business processes in getting started with clean core achievement for your enterprise systems.

Our aim is to provide you with a set of guiding principles for building resilient business processes based on industry standards and justify differentiating extensions, supported by seamless integration, reliable data quality and efficient operations. This is laid out in the four chapters of the white paper which are as follows:

  • Introduction to clean core strategy
  • Importance of business processes in the SAP clean core strategy
  • Fit to Standard in SAP S/4HANA Cloud
  • Getting started with Fit to Standard.

 

What is the importance of business processes in SAP’s “clean core” strategy?

A clean digital core provides flexibility and enables scale for changing business requirements, is easier to maintain and upgrade, and has a stable design on which latest solution capabilities and innovations can be adopted with relative ease.

The clean core concept has multiple principles, namely: Business Processes, Extensibility, Data, Integration and Operations.

In this blog post, we will focus on the principle of business processes and the importance of reducing complexity by increasing adherence to industry standards as a starting point for transformations. We shall also present the importance of treating business process management as a long term organizational best practice, the process to be followed when standard processes do not fulfil business value objectives, as well as the integrated tool chain recommendations for managing business processes.

 

How process standardisation supports “clean core” achievement in transformations

Today’s transformation methodologies need to ensure that business process management (BPM) is a continuous organizational practice that goes hand in hand with system transformations, as well as continuous improvements to, and regular operations of, those systems.

A good BPM practice delivers:

 

SAP Activate, therefore, integrates BPM throughout its phases to ensure standardization and best use of the capabilities and innovations in SAP S/4HANA Cloud. This is driven by the Fit to Standard (F2S) mindset deeply embedded in SAP Activate.

In chapter 2 of this white paper, we provide detailed guidance on the role of process standardization in modern transformations across all phases of the SAP Activate methodology.

 

How to achieve process standardization – Fit to Standard

Fit to Standard is a fundamental new way of thinking in the cloud era. It provides the ability to rapidly adopt standard best practices and leverage cloud technology to establish a foundation for engaging in continuous innovation. A well-defined fit to standard approach enables an organization to achieve higher agility, flexibility and collaboration, thereby optimizing the use of business and technology innovations that the cloud offers.

The RISE with SAP Methodology provides clear guidance on how clean core adherence should be driven by the fit to standard approach, and the role that proper governance (through a well-functioning solution standardization board) plays in transformations. More details on fit to standard approach and accelerators, requirement and gap management, as well as, the solution standardization board, can be found in chapter 3 of the white paper. SAP also provides maturity assessment and jumpstart services for solution standardization board in your program through Design and Value Governance service.

 

How to Get Started?

First and foremost, read our white paper on Clean Core business processes for SAP S/4HANA Cloud. The last chapter of the document details the four-step path to getting started with a fit to standard approach:

  1. Focus on mindset and adopt the right methodology and tools
  2. Nail down the scope and identify the standards available
  3. Identify value creation opportunities through process standardization and innovation adoption
  4. Establish solution standardization board to govern deviations from standard and ensure that every gap extension is justified appropriately and linked to value creation opportunities.

Chapter 4 of this white paper also provides detailed information and links to understand the role that the following tools in the integrated tool chain plays in transformation execution as prescribed in the RISE with SAP Methodology:

  • SAP LeanIX
  • SAP Signavio
  • SAP CALM

Now you're ready to begin your fruitful journey to achieve Clean Core by leveraging Clean Core business processes for SAP S/4HANA Cloud ensure that you have maximized the adoption of standard industry processes and focussed your energies on achieving a clean extensibility in those processes where you can truly differentiate from your peers and competitors. For more details on continuing your journey to clean core, you should also refer to the Clean core extensibility for SAP S/4HANA Cloud white paper.

entry.title: How to Convert Retail Site Master from SAP ERP to S/4HANA - Updated
entry.content:
Introduction

Transitioning from SAP ERP to S/4HANA is like upgrading from a reliable old car to a sleek, high-performance vehicle. It’s faster, smarter, and packed with new features. If you are using Retail industry solution, one of the key tasks in this journey is converting the Retail Site Master—a cornerstone of retail operations—into the S/4HANA environment. This blog will walk you through the process, breaking it down into simple, actionable steps.

Harmonization of Site Master and Business Partner (BP)

In SAP ERP, the Retail Site Master is the go-to object for managing retail locations, storing everything from addresses to sales data. However, S/4HANA introduces a more streamlined and integrated approach. By converting the Retail Site Master, you’re not just moving data—you’re aligning your retail operations with the modern, simplified architecture of S/4HANA.

Before diving into the conversion, it’s important to understand how S/4HANA handles retail sites. In S/4HANA, the Retail Site Master is harmonized with the Business Partner concept. This means your retail sites will now be treated as Business Partners, enabling better integration with other modules like Finance, Sales, and Logistics.

 

In S/4HANA, the Business Partner (BP) is the central entity for managing customers (C) and vendors (V). Key changes include:

  • Mandatory BP Integration: Sites must now link to a BP with C/V roles.
  • Address Data Harmonization: Address data is maintained via BP, not directly in the site.
  • Elimination of Redundancies: Departments, receiving points, and geolocation data are managed through BP roles.

Impacted Processes:

  • Site maintenance, distribution, and archiving.
  • Transactions WB01/WB02/WB03 now integrate BP maintenance.

 Preparation for Conversion

Data Cleanup

  • Remove obsolete sites and archive inactive ones (use transaction WB06 and SAP Note 690340).
  • Resolve data conflicts between C/V assigned to the same site (e.g., tax numbers, industries, bank details).

Customizing Activities

  • Activate CVI Synchronization:
    • Path: Cross-Application Components → Master Data Synchronization → Synchronization Control.
    • Enable synchronization directions BP → Customer and BP → Vendor.
  • Define BP Groupings and Number Ranges:
    • Assign external/internal numbering to BP groupings (table TB001).
    • Ensure number ranges are sufficient (pre-check ID 019).
  • Configure BP Roles:
    • Create BP roles with category BPSITE (default role ID: BPSITE). Use BAdI BADI_CHANGE_BP_NUMBER to rename roles if conflicts exist (SAP Note 2315639).
  • Implement BAdIs:
    • Adjust BP numbering logic via BADI_CHANGE_BP_NUMBER (methods CHANGE_BP_NUMBER and CHANGE_BP_ROLE_NAME_BPSITE).

Pre-Check Execution

Run the preparation report PERFORM_CHK_SMBP_FOR_S4_SIC (SAP Note 2502552) to validate:

  • Critical Errors (e.g., missing C/V account groups, conflicting BP roles).
  • Warnings (e.g., inconsistent tax numbers, duplicate BP assignments).
  • Use transaction SM_CVI_FS_CHECK_CUS to resolve CVI Customizing issues (SAP Note 2451504).

Conversion Process

Pre-Check Validation

Ensure no critical errors (type E) remain. Key checks include:

  • Site Data Consistency:
    • Check ID 001: Sites without C/V assigned.
    • Check ID 063: Tax number conflicts between C/V.
  • BP Customizing:
    • Check ID 010/011: Missing BP groupings for C/V account groups.

Execute XPRAs

  • WFIL_XPRA_S4MIG_TWRF2: Updates site profiles (table TWRF2).
  • WFIL_XPRA_S4_ADJUST_DISTR_TABS: Adjusts distribution tables (removes C/V/address tables).

Run Site Migration Report

  • Report: WFIL_CSR_S4MIG_CV_BP (SAP Note 2429142).
  • Key Actions:
    • Creates BP for each site with roles BPSITE, FLCU00 (C), and FLVN00 (V).
    • Migrates contact persons to BP relationships.
  • Log Analysis: Use UPGBA_DISPLAY_REPORT_LOG (log ID MIGRATION_CV_BP) to resolve issues.

Post-Conversion Activities

Resolve Conversion Issues

  • Missing BPs: Re-run migration report after fixing errors.
  • PPO Requests: Use transaction MDS_PPO2 to handle synchronization errors.

Update Customizing

  • Site Profiles: Assign BP groupings (e.g., S_CUYN for sites with C/V roles).
  • DRF Configuration: Set up Data Replication Framework for site distribution (transaction DRFIMG).

Site Maintenance in S/4HANA

  • Integrated BP Maintenance: Use transaction WB02 to assign/modify BP roles.
  • Address Management: Addresses are maintained via BP (transaction BP).

Archiving

  • Use transaction WB06 to set archiving markers (BP archiving is blocked until the site is archived).

Key SAP Notes and References

  • SAP Note 2310884: Site conversion guide and pre-check details.
  • SAP Note 2339008: Deprecated transactions (e.g., WRFCATEGORYSHOP).
  • SAP Note 2265093: General BP conversion guidelines.

entry.title: How to Release PR document from HOLD or PARK status using BAPI
entry.content:
Introduction:

A purchase requisition is an internal document used in an organization to request the procurement of goods or services. In SAP, efficient management of purchase requisitions is crucial for smooth procurement operations. This blog will provide a clear guide on releasing PR documents from HOLD or PARK status using BAPIs and releasing the document for further processing.

 

Understanding Parked and Hold Documents in SAP:

First, let's understand the PARK and HOLD options in SAP. These functionalities are essential for managing purchasing documents, allowing users to save them temporarily for later processing.

Hold: If the document has incorrect or incomplete data and you want to save it temporarily to process later, use the Hold option.

Park: If the document has complete and correct data but needs further workflow approvals or budget checks, use the Park functionality.

 

Activating Hold and Park options:

To activate the PARK and HOLD options in transactions ME51N, ME52N, and ME53N, follow these two steps:

  1. Activate Business Functionality
    • Go to T-Code SPRO.
    • Click on Activate Business Functions

       

    • Expand the folder Enterprise_Business_Functions and activate the functionality LOG_MM_CI_3.

       

     2. Enable PARK and HOLD Features In T-Code SPRO, follow this path:
          Materials Management --> Purchasing --> Environment Data --> Activate 'Park and Hold' in Purchasing Documents. 

 By completing these steps, you will enable the PARK and HOLD functionalities for purchasing documents in your SAP system.

 

Changing PR document status from HOLD or PARKED to SAVED using BAPI:

To change the status of a purchase requisition document from HOLD or PARKED to SAVED, you can use the BAPI “BAPI_PR_CHANGE.”. 

Below is an example of how to implement this in ABAP:

 

 

DATA: ls_prheader  TYPE bapimereqheader,
      ls_prheaderx TYPE bapimereqheaderx,
      lt_return    TYPE TABLE OF bapiret2.

IF  ls_prheader-preq_no IS NOT INITIAL.

 ls_prheader = VALUE #( preq_no       = |{ ls_prheader-preq_no ALPHA = IN }|
                        park_complete = abap_false
                        hold_complete = abap_false
                        memorytype    = abap_false ).
  
 ls_prheaderx = VALUE #( preq_no       = abap_true
                         park_complete = abap_true
                         hold_complete = abap_true
                         memorytype    = abap_true ).

CALL FUNCTION 'BAPI_PR_CHANGE'
  EXPORTING
   number     = ls_prheader-preq_no “Your PR Number
    prheader  = ls_prheader
    prheaderx = ls_prheaderx
  TABLES
    return   = lt_return.
IF VALUE #( lt_return[ type = 'S' ] OPTIONAL ) IS NOT INITIAL.
  CALL FUNCTION 'BAPI_TRANSACTION_COMMIT'
    EXPORTING
       wait = abap_true.
ELSE.
    ROLLBACK WORK.
ENDIF.
ENDIF.

 

 

 

 

Release Strategy:

The release strategy in SAP defines the approval process for purchase requisitions:

  • Purpose: Ensures PRs are reviewed and approved before conversion to purchase orders.
  • Release Codes: Each strategy can have up to eight release codes, indicating who must approve the requisition.
  • Approval Sequence: Specifies the order in which approvals must be obtained.
  • Assignment: Release strategies are assigned based on release conditions like document type and value.

 

Release Code:

A release code is a two-character ID that authorizes a person to approve or release a purchase requisition or external purchasing document.

  • Purpose: It serves as an electronic signature, replacing traditional handwritten approvals. This digitization streamlines the approval process and enhances efficiency.
  • Assignment: Release codes are assigned to employees based on their roles and authorization levels within the organization. For example, a clerk might have a release code for initial approvals, while a manager has a higher-level code.
  • Configuration:
      • You can define up to eight release codes within a release strategy.
      • Each code is linked to specific approval levels, ensuring that the correct personnel review PRs based on their value or other criteria.
  • Approval Process: The release strategy specifies which release codes must be used and the sequence of approvals required.

 

Releasing PR documents using BAPI:

To release a PR that needs approval from respective employees/approvers in your organization, you can use the BAPI - “BAPI_REQUISITION_RELEASE_GEN”.

Below is an example implementation:

 

 

 

DATA : lt_return TYPE TABLE OF bapireturn.
 
 CALL FUNCTION 'BAPI_REQUISITION_RELEASE_GEN'
   EXPORTING
     number     = iv_pr_num       “Your PR Number
     rel_code   = iv_release_code “Release code
   TABLES
     return      = lt_return.
 IF VALUE #( lt_return[ type = 'S' ] OPTIONAL ) IS NOT INITIAL OR 
    lt_return IS INITIAL.
  CALL FUNCTION 'BAPI_TRANSACTION_COMMIT'
    EXPORTING
       wait = abap_true.
 ELSE.
    ROLLBACK WORK.
 ENDIF.

 

 

 

 

Finding Release Codes:

You can find release codes under the Release Strategy tab in transactions ME51N, ME52N, and ME53N.

The following tables are useful for retrieving relevant information:

  • EBAN: To get the release group and release strategy for the PR number.
  • T16FS: To get release codes for a particular release group & strategy.
  • T16FW: To get approver names/IDs for a release code.

 

Conclusion:

This blog emphasized the significance of parked and hold documents, the activation of essential functionalities, and the use of BAPIs to change PR statuses. We also explored the release procedure and strategy, illustrating how structured approvals can enhance efficiency in your procurement processes.

Thank you for reading!

entry.title: Deactivating Simplified Sourcing in SAP S/4HANA
entry.content:
SAP S/4HANA introduced several simplifications in Production Planning, especially for Material Requirements Planning (MRP), as I explained in my blog MRP: Simplifications in S/4HANA. One of these simplifications is known as Simplified Sourcing, and it changes the logic used by MRP to determine the source of supply for a replenishment proposal with external procurement.

Some of the changes introduced by Simplified Sourcing in the MRP source determination were:

  • Source List is no longer mandatory for source determination.
  • Checking the new flag in “Auto Sourcing” in the info record is enough to make a source of supply relevant for MRP.
  • Contracts and Scheduling Agreements are automatically selected by MRP as a source of supply, if they exist.

SAP note 2268069 explains the simplified sourcing logic in detail, and it suggests that it is simpler than classic MRP because of the following reasons:

  • The different sources of supply are pretty symmetrical. Sources of supply have a temporal validity, a quantitative validity, a deletion indicator, and a "relevant for automatic sourcing" indicator. The sourcing logic can treat the different sources of supply in a uniform way
  • Quota arrangements can be used to prioritize any of the possible sources of supply
  • Purchasing info records are proper sources of supply. The SAP S/4HANA MRP can select purchasing info records without source list entries. It is sufficient to define the supplier information of a material in the purchasing info record.
  • Production versions make sure BOM and routing fit together
  • Production versions have always been the only in-house production source of supply integrated with APO PP/DS. With production versions as the only in-house production source of supply, ERP sourcing and PP/DS sourcing become more similar.
  • Purchasing info records, contracts, and delivery schedules have always been the external procurement sources of supply integrated with APO. With purchasing info records being proper sources of supply, ERP sourcing and PP/DS sourcing become more similar
  • A unified source of supply model is the basis for future innovations in sourcing

 

While simplified sourcing indeed streamlines the master data required for source determination, it brings one additional challenge to companies that are migrating from SAP ECC to SAP S/4HANA, as they will need to review the existing master data, in order to make sure that source determination works in SAP S/4HANA.

In order to minimize the migration efforts, SAP implemented a new setting that allows simplified sourcing to be deactivated for external procurement. This new logic can be introduced into all the supported SAP S/4HANA releases with the following notes:

3552667 - Feature: Disable/Enable simplified sourcing in S/4HANA for plants - Dynpro changes
3493681 - Feature: Disable/Enable simplified sourcing in S/4HANA for plants.

SAP Note 3493681 introduces the new flag 'SKIP_SIMPLIFIED_SOURCING' in table T399D, and when this flag is checked, then only the source list is considered by both MRP and MRP Live as a valid source of supply.

SAP Note 3552667 implements the screen changes and it brings the field 'Skip Simplified Sourcing' into customizing. This flag can be accessed either through customizing transaction OMDT (external procurement customizing) or OPPQ (plant parameters customizing). Figure 1 shows the setting checked in view Default Values for Purchasing/MRP.

Figure 1: Skip Simplified Sourcing

 

 

Now let’s see some examples of how exactly this flag works in MRP and observe the MRP behavior with different combination of settings.

Example 1:

  • Flag Skip Simplified Sourcing Unchecked
  • Auto Sourcing Checked in the Info Record
  • No Source List

In this example we will just simulate the standard Simplified Sourcing logic, because the new flag Skip Simplified Sourcing is unchecked. Figure 2 shows that the flag Auto Sourcing is checked in the Info Record of supplier VENDOR_001 for material S4HANA_ROH_005.

Figure 2: Auto Sourcing checked in the Info Record

 

After running MRP, we can see in transaction that this supplier has been selected as a valid source of supply for a purchase requisition created by MRP, because the Simplified Sourcing logic was used by MRP.

Figure 3: Source of Supply determined by MRP

 

Now let’s go to the next example, where we will deactivate Simplified Sourcing and run MRP again, with the same master data.

Example 1:

  • Flag Skip Simplified Sourcing Checked
  • Auto Sourcing Checked in the Info Record
  • No Source List

In the new example we kept the same master data previously used, but we have checked the flag Skip Simplified Sourcing in the MRP customizing.

Figure 4: Skip Simplified Sourcing Checked in customizing

 

When we run MRP for the same material and with an identical planning situation, we can observe that MRP could not determine a vendor for the purchase requisition. That happened because we don’t have a source list for our material, and with the Simplified Sourcing logic deactivated, the Source List becomes mandatory for the MRP Source Determination.

Note that the system behavior will be the same if we run Classic MRP  or MRP Live.

 

Figure 5: Source of Supply was not determined by MRP, because there is no Source List

 

Now let’s see another example, where we will keep Simplified Sourcing deactivated and we will create a Source List.

Example 3:

  • Flag Skip Simplified Sourcing Checked
  • Auto Sourcing Checked in the Info Record for VENDOR_001
  • Source List created where VENDOR_002 is relevant to MRP.

In this third example we will keep the Skip Simplified Sourcing flag checked, and we will create a Source List, but the supplier relevant to MRP will be VENDOR_002, for which the Auto Sourcing flag is unchecked in the Info Record.

Figure 6: Source List with a valid Source of Supply

 

After we run MRP for our material, we can observe in MD04 that VENDOR_002 has been selected as the purchase requisition source of supply, as it is relevant for MRP in the source list.

Figure 7: Source of Supply determined by MRP

 

 

In the examples above we have demonstrated the source determination logic using only Info Records and Source Lists, but the results would be similar when using a Contract or Scheduling agreement:

  • If the Skip Simplified Sourcing is checked, Contracts and Scheduling Agreements will be automatically selected as a source of supply.
  • If the Skip Simplified Sourcing is unchecked, Contracts and Scheduling Agreements will only be selected as a source of supply if there is a source list entry determining that they should be selected by MRP.

 

 

Brought to you by the SAP S/4HANA RIG

entry.title: SAP Document and Reporting Compliance for Singapore GST InvoiceNow
entry.content:
GST InvoiceNow - submission of invoice data to Singapore Tax Authority (IRAS)

Image from IRAS GST InvoiceNow page

On April 15, 2024, The Inland Revenue Authority of Singapore (IRAS) published media release "Implementation of InvoiceNow for GST-Registered Businesses and free InvoiceNow Services for Newly In..." in the Newsroom. GST-registered businesses will be required to use InvoiceNow solutions to transmit the invoice data directly to IRAS for tax administration. By transmitting invoice data directly to IRAS via the InvoiceNow solutions, GST- registered businesses can streamline their compliance processes, reduce data preparation efforts for their submissions to IRAS and enjoy faster GST refunds.

GST InvoiceNow requirement would be implemented in phases, starting with soft launch from May 2025, allowing any existing GST-registered businesses that wish to be early adopters to transmit invoice data to IRAS using InvoiceNow solutions via InvoiceNow network.

  • From 1 May 2025, for GST-registered businesses that wish to be early adopters.
  • From 1 November 2025, for newly incorporated companies[1] that voluntarily apply for GST registration.
  • From 1 April 2026, for all businesses that voluntarily apply for GST registration.

IRAS would continue to consult industry partners on the initiative and carefully review the feedback received before announcing further details for the remaining GST-registered businesses. 

InvoiceNow solutions are finance and accounting software that allow the transmission of invoices between businesses via the InvoiceNow network. InvoiceNow solutions are connected to IRAS through Access Points (AP). This connection would enable the transmission of invoice data to IRAS via Application Programming Interface (API) technology. 

Invoices that are not issued using InvoiceNow (e.g. for sales made via point-of-sale systems) should also be recorded in InvoiceNow solutions and sent to IRAS.

The scope of invoice data covers the transactions with GST tax categories of: -

(1) Standard-rate supplies,

(2) Zero-rated supplies,

(3) Standard-rated purchases on which input tax claims are made or will be made.

For (1) & (3) excludes reverse charge supplies and reverse charge purchases.

For more information on the GST InvoiceNow requirement, please check to the IRAS GST InvoiceNow Requirement webpage here.

SAP is an InvoiceNow solution for both Access Point Provider and InvoiceNow-Ready Solution Provider and is currently developing SAP Document and Reporting solution to support GST InvoiceNow requirement, please check the plan & update at regulatory change manager 

Once the solution is ready, the how-to-implement will be published here.

Get to know about Peppol eInvoices or InvoiceNow solution in Singapore, please check the blogs: -

SAP Document and Reporting Compliance for Singapore Peppol eInvoices (InvoiceNow) - Part 1

SAP Document and Reporting Compliance for Singapore Peppol eInvoices (InvoiceNow) - Part 2

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

This blog is intended to provide Customers and Partners the guide or how-to implement SAP Document and Reporting Compliance, cloud edition for Singapore GST InvoiceNow requirement.

Please share your feedback and thoughts in a comment. If you have questions about SAP Document and Reporting Compliance, you can post your question here.

entry.title: Customer Engagement Initiative 2025 on Business Event Logging
entry.content:
As part of the new CEI cycle for 2025 we launched a CEI project for Business Event Logging. Registration is open until March 28, 2025.

Business Event Logging allows you to create event logs in SAP S/4HANA systems and on SAP BTP ABAP Environment, based on Business Events and on Change Documents. The logs can be used to evaluate changes to your business documents, and to analyze your business processes with the help of SAP Signavio Process Intelligence.

Register here until March 28, 2025: Insights into Business Processes with Business Event Logging 

We plan to roll-out information about the usage of Business Event Logging, and collect your feedback and ideas.

Find more information about Business Event Logging here:

  • One Tool to Track Them All 
  • Using Customer-Defined Events in Business Event Logging
  • Integrating Business Event Logging with SAP Signavio
  • Transform your Business Processes with SAP Signavio and SAP Build 

Looking forward to seeing you there!

entry.title: Key User Custom Fields in S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition and On-Premise: Dos and Don’ts
entry.content:
Teaser

Did you ever wonder how transporting key user custom fields works in S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition and On-Premise and which apps you should use to avoid common mistakes? 
Do you want to know what to consider regarding key user custom fields when you upgrade your S/4HANA system? 
If your answer is yes, then this blog post is just for you. 

 

Disclaimer

Please note that this blog does exclusively address SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition and SAP S/4HANA On-Premise and does not address SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition. 
In this blog we use S/4HANA as an abbreviation for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition and SAP S/4HANA On-Premise. In addition, for key user custom fields we use the abbreviation custom fields.

 

Table of Contents

This blog post covers the following topics: 

  • Dos and Don’ts in the context of software logistics
  • What to consider regarding custom fields in upgrade situations 
  • General Dos and Don’ts regarding custom fields 
  • Dos and Don’ts regarding ABAP-managed fields (abbreviation AMF) 

 

Dos and Don’ts in the context of software logistics

When working with key user custom fields in S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, you use "software collections" to transport your custom fields. These collections act like a safety box, wrapping the technical transport process. This is different in S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition and On-Premise. There, you transport the custom field artifacts on your own using a standard transport mechanism. With this, you have more freedom, but there are many things that could go wrong. It can easily happen that the managed and unmanaged objects of a custom field aren't kept in sync, by forgetting to transport the unmanaged objects, for example. 

Here is a list of Dos SAP recommends: 

  • Use Fiori apps for transport handling (also refer to note 2660797)
    • Register Extensions for Transport to
      • assign key user items to a package 
      • assign custom fields to a transport 
    • Configure Software Packages to  
      • configure automatic transport or task handling for packages 
      • assign transports to packages 
  • Register a so-called job user for generation processes (also refer to SAP Help Portal). You must ensure that: 
    • the job user is provided in maintenance view ATO_PK_JOB_USER (transaction SM30) 
    • the job user has sufficient authorization for publishing (e.g. SAP_ALL profile) 
    • the job user is valid (exists in the correct client and is within validity range) 
    • operations (save/publish/delete) are performed in the correct client 

And here is a list of Don’ts: 

  • Never develop custom fields in several different development systems – use one development system only 
    • Implemented checks are system-specific. This means that no data of other systems is considered 
    • Generated artifact names depend on custom field names, which means that a clash of object names may occur 
  • Never edit custom fields in several systems. Only edit them in one development system: 
    • Custom fields and its generated artifacts have a dedicated source system. This means that there is a risk of system inconsistency in case of custom field deletion if generated objects exist only locally 

What to consider regarding custom fields in upgrade situations

There is one more crucial difference regarding lifecycle stability for custom fields when working in S/4HANA compared to S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition. In S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, the upgrade procedure contains a task where custom field artifacts are technically adapted on the fly, due to changes in the SAP standard.  
This is different from the upgrade procedure in S/4HANA. In S/4HANA, there is a custom field analysis process figuring out whether an adaptation is necessary. If this process identifies an adaptation need, you'll see the following messages in the upgrade log: 

Message message id 'CFD_REGENERATE_OP' message number '001' ('Regeneration of Custom fields necessary; refer to SAP Note 2640715') 

Please note that the following message could be shown, too: 

Message message id 'CFD_REGENERATE_OP' message number '016' ('Regeneration of datasource extension necessary; refer to SAP Note 2640715') 

This second message tells you that the issue is not an issue with a custom field but rather with a key user datasource extension object. You can find more details in note 2640715.  

Please note that there's a difference between upgrading the development system or a test or production system. 

In case you are upgrading the development system and an adaptation need has been identified, you should run the following report: CFD_REGENERATE_IN_ONPREMISE. This report implements the needed adaptations. When you use it, make sure to always be on the newest note level of this report. 

In case you are upgrading a system different from the development system, and an adaptation need has been identified, don't run the report. Instead, import the adaptations which have been made in the development system. 

In some scenarios, things can get a bit more complicated. For instance, you might want to run a test upgrade. To do this, you copy the development system, giving it a different system ID. Alternatively, you might need to move your development system to another system, resulting in a new system ID. In these cases, it's important to note that custom fields are only editable in their original source system. They are not editable in other systems. To enable their editability, you have to follow note 3484189.  
Only do this in the exceptional situations described above. Never maintain your custom fields in more than one system. If you maintain your custom fields in more than one system, they will break. 

 

General Dos and Don’ts regarding custom fields 

There may be situations where you created a custom field with a certain name (custom field ID) and accidentally deleted it. Now, you want to re-create the custom field with exactly the same name of the deleted one. 
This can be done. First, check the context you're in: 

  • If the transport with the field creation has not been released, you can delete it and recreate the field, even when choosing an incompatible field type. 
  • If the transport with the field creation has been released, you can delete it and recreate the field only as long as the field type you chose is compatible (see also note 3328541) 

It may happen that you want to expose your custom field in an OData service (to make the field visible in a UI that gets its data from this OData service, for example) and this OData service can't be enabled in the Custom Fields app. In this case, the respective OData service is not enabled for custom fields. To solve this, you can create a respective extensibility enabling request via the customer influence channel. 
Please note that using an OData redefinition approach to add custom fields to OData service entities doesn't work and is not supported (Note 1574568 constraint 34). 

 

Dos and Don’ts in the context of ABAP-managed fields 

These Dos and Don'ts are relevant for you if you use the feature ABAP-Managed Fields (described based on a concrete example in the blog post Convert Manually Created Append Fields to Real Key User Custom Fields). ABAP-managed fields allow you to convert your manually created append fields into real key user custom fields.
The challenges regarding software logistics come from the fact that for ABAP-managed fields, some of the custom fields' artefacts are generated and managed by the Custom Fields framework, and some are customer-managed (such as the CDS Code View, the CDS Text View, data elements, append, and so on). As these customer-managed artifacts are not safe-guarded and controlled by the Custom Fields framework, one could easily break the custom field by changing these manually created objects in an incompatible way, or accidentally deleting them.  

Are ABAP-managed fields lifecycle-stable, and do they follow the clean core principles?

The answer is yes. Let's have a closer look at the details.  
An ABAP-managed field comes with a set of unmanaged objects: 

  • Data element and domain (manually created or SAP standard data element) 
  • Manually created append with the field type using the data element above 
  • Depending on the chosen semantic type, also a manually created CDS code view and CDS text view  

If all of these unmanaged objects are built with ABAP language version ABAP for Cloud Development, they automatically apply to the clean core rules. The ABAP-managed field is 100% clean core compliant and fully lifecycle-stable. 

In general, within your unmanaged objects, it's possible that you use non-C1 released objects. With that, you can't fully follow the clean core path any longer. 

Let's dive a little deeper into the unmanaged objects to better understand how critical certain things are in terms of upgrade stability: 

Data element and domain

If the data element or domain you use to type your ABAP-managed field is not an SAP standard object which is released for Use in Cloud Development, then this data element or domain could be changed incompatibly or even deleted with any updated version of S/4HANA. However, in a lot of cases it’s unlikely that this will happen because, for example, the data element is prominently used within the SAP standard (which means that it's used for the typing of key fields.) 

Append field

In some cases, the Extension Include Structure to which you add the field using an ABAP dictionary append is released for Extensibility in Cloud Development. Then, you can add this append in ABAP language version ABAP for Cloud Development. In case the Extension Include Structure is not released for developers but only for key users, you can't add the DDIC append in ABAP language version ABAP for Cloud Development but only in Standard ABAP. This no longer completely follows the clean core principle, but can still be 100% upgrade-stable, as it’s protected with the C0-contract release for key users. Using ABAP language version Standard, there are no more lifecycle stability checks in place to prevent you from leaving the upgrade-stable way. Consider the following aspects to be 100% upgrade-stable: 

  • Make sure that you use ZZ as prefix of the field name, or your own customer namespace to avoid collisions with future standard fields or your own custom fields 
  • Make sure that you’re using the field suffix as defined in the respective business context in transaction SCFD_REGISTRY

CDS Code View and CDS Text View

In case your CDS view references SAP standard objects which are not released for Use in Cloud Development (CDS views, data elements, database tables, …) these objects may be changed incompatibly or even deleted with an updated version of S/4HANA. In this case, 100% upgrade stability is no longer guaranteed. 

In the worst case, if one of the non-C1-released SAP standard objects you reference has been changed incompatibly or has been deleted, it can cause issues during a system upgrade. You’d have to rework your affected unmanaged objects to solve these issues. 

You can get more information regarding ABAP-managed fields following these links:  

  • General handling: SAP note 2981484 
  • Enable ABAP-managed fields for database tables with proxy views: SAP note 2463257 
  • Re-creating a custom field: SAP note 3327309 

Golden rules when working with ABAP-managed fields

  • Use the ATO apps for transport handling (also refer to note 2660797) 
  • Only use ABAP-managed fields in the exceptional situations described above 
  • Use a suitable prefix for the append field (ZZ or a real namespace) 
  • Use a suitable suffix for the append field depending on the business context 
  • Ensure that these objects are kept in the same software component as the append structure with the database field itself 
  • Ensure that the append field also exists in subsequent systems. Either the append field is imported before or transported together with the custom field 
  • Never delete append fields via SE11 before disabling the custom field via SCFD_EUI 
  • Never modify or delete the value help view used in the AMF field of type List 
  • Never delete the data element used in the AMF field 
  • Never continue with the publishing or deletion of further custom fields if a previous publishing or deletion failed 

Now, you are done! Congratulations! With this, you are now equipped to 

  • handle software logistics for custom fields 
  • avoid common pitfalls in this context. 

Please note that this blog has been elaborated by a team of experts, and I want to 
thank Yasmina van Rooij (YasminavanRooij), Daniel Wachs (DanielWachs), and Georg Wilhelm (georg_wilhelm) for their extremely helpful contributions.

 

Feedback, Comments, or Questions

You’re cordially invited to provide feedback, comments, or questions in the comments section below. Please follow our profiles to get updates when we publish more posts on these topics. 
Feel invited to post and answer questions here (https://answers.sap.com/tags/270c4f37-c335-46e1-bfad-a256637d5e26) and read other posts on the topic (https://blogs.sap.com/tags/270c4f37-c335-46e1-bfad-a256637d5e26/). 

Other related blog posts: 

  1. Convert Manually Created Append Fields to Real Key User Custom Fields 
  2. How to implement a custom field of type “Code List based on CDS view” with a developer extensibility custom entity 
    How to implement a custom entity in the ABAP RESTful Programming Model using remote function modules    
  3. How to configure the value help as a context-dependent value help 
  4. How to configure intent-based navigation on a key user custom field of type “Code List based on CDS View”    
    and in this case, how to easily find the fitting semantic object and semantic object parameter: Custom field type “Code list based on CDS view”: Finding/defining the right value help view and the right Semantic Object and Semantic Object Parameter for Intent-Based Navigation 
  5. Custom Fields in S/4HANA: How to control field properties like mandatory, read-only, and visibility 
  6. Key User Custom Fields in SAP S/4HANA SAP GUI UIs: How to control field properties (mandatory,...) 
  7. Key User Custom Fields in Fiori UIs based on SEGW OData Services: How to control field properties 
  8. Key User Custom Fields in Fiori UIs based on OData Services exposed with Service Binding 
  9. Custom fields in S/4HANA: Key User versus Developer Extensibility 
  10. SAP Screen Personas: How to position key user custom fields exactly where you want them to be

entry.title: Are you familar with the key configure and use key QM processes?
entry.content:
I recommend to access our Configuring SAP S/4HANA Quality Management learning journey.

Overview
This learning journey will introduce you to the advanced concepts of SAP S/4HANA Quality Management (QM) focusing on the creation and management of basic data, inspection planning, quality notifications, the complete quality inspection lifecycle, and quality management costs. 

Learning objectives
At the end of this learning journey, consultants will be able to:

  1. Create basic data, including setting up sample determination, dynamic modification, master inspection characteristics, and codes.
  2. Develop and enhance comprehensive inspection plans using basic data.
  3. Configure result recording, valuation, and usage decisions within SAP S/4HANA Quality Management.
  4. Configure and process quality notifications across different operational processes, such as procurement, sales, and production, facilitating effective defect recording and cost management.
  5. Record Quality Management costs using the QM order.

Prerequisites

  • Applying SAP S/4HANA Quality Management

Practice your skills in a live SAP environment

Please ask a question related to the digital learning Journey in the Q&A area. 

Our SAP Learning Experts will get back to you as soon as possible! We are here to support you. 

I appreciate your feedback and we will make sure to continue sharing interesting topics.

Kind regards
Margit

entry.title: How Data driven Change Management with Deep Transformation Insights ensures successful SAP Business
entry.content:
This blog highlights the critical role of Organizational Change Management (OCM) in the successful implementation of SAP S/4HANA. It delves into how SAP's Deep Transformation Insights (DTI) service leverages data-driven insights to help organizations manage change effectively, ensuring employee acceptance and engagement throughout the transformation process.

The Challenge of SAP S/4HANA Implementations: A Change Management Perspective

Implementing SAP S/4HANA is a transformative process that goes beyond a mere technological upgrade. It reshapes business operations, IT systems, and employee behavior while embracing a Fit-to-Standard mindset This increased focus on standards helps to reduce the time and costs associated with implementation and support, and also drives business efficiencies as well as scalability. It is SAP’s recommended approach to enable faster adoption of SAP S/4HANA.

Despite the recognized importance of Organizational Change Management (OCM) in ensuring successful business transformation, many organizations struggle to integrate it effectively into their strategy. We recently did a Change Assessment with more than 800 respondents on the perception of different important OCM related dimensions. See here some highlights:

Key Challenges in OCM Implementations

Strategic Alignment and Ownership

  • Confusion about who should take ownership of OCM within the organization
  • Lack of clarity in defining desired business outcomes
  • Misconceptions about OCM's scope and responsibilities

Leadership Involvement

  • 64% of companies acknowledge senior leaders' crucial role in driving change
  • Only 56% report active engagement of leaders with their teams
  • Lack of visible support undermines morale and alignment

Communication and Change Story

  • Only 48% of organizations actively communicate SAP program updates
  • 54% have a clearly defined Change Story

Resistance to change evolves when discussions focus around Fit-Gap keeping the current business processes in the future system and NOT focusing on fit-to-standard.

Past Experiences and Skepticism

  • Poor outcomes in previous OCM efforts lead to skepticism
  • Some organizations exclude OCM from new projects due to past experiences

Resource Constraints and ROI Measurement

  • Lack of internal structures and expertise for effective OCM implementation
  • 71% believe their Change Management Strategy explains the SAP program's importance
  • Only 47% feel end users are equipped to apply SAP solutions in daily work
  • OCM is often viewed as an optional expense rather than a critical success factor
  • Difficulty in justifying additional spending on OCM
  • Only 44% of organizations actively assess the effectiveness of their change initiatives
  • Lack of clear metrics to demonstrate ROI

Overcoming these challenges requires a holistic approach to OCM, emphasizing clear ownership, leadership involvement, effective communication, and measurable outcomes. By addressing these key areas, organizations can maximize the benefits of their SAP S/4HANA implementation and ensure a smoother transition for all stakeholders.

To address such challenges the Deep Transformation Insights (DTI) service from SAP comes into play. With this data driven approach, organizations can safeguard the success of their SAP S/4HANA implementations. It's not just about deploying new technology; it's about preparing the people who will use it, ensuring sustainable adoption, and unlocking the full potential of the transformation.

DTI is built around five key dimensions or sentiments—Awareness, Acceptance, Enablement, Empowerment, and Commitment—that provide a structured path for guiding individuals through change, fostering both readiness and long-term engagement. Let’s look at these briefly.

Research-based Change Sentiments to Accelerate Change and Adoption in SAP Implementations

Based on research conducted by organizational scientists like A. Armenakis, this five important factors have been identified as they determine the degree of employee buy-in by organizational change recipients.

Awareness

  • Foundation of successful transformation
  • Ensures recognition of the need for change
  • Focuses on communicating the "why" behind the change
  • Aligns change initiative with broader business goals

Acceptance

  • Builds on awareness
  • Addresses emotional and intellectual readiness
  • Focuses on trust-building and reducing resistance
  • Creates psychological safety for employees

Enablement

  • Bridge between readiness and action
  • Equips individuals with necessary tools, training, and resources
  • Creates a supportive environment for adapting to new roles
  • Minimizes disruption and maximizes productivity during transformation

Empowerment

  • Fosters ownership and confidence among employees
  • Grants authority for decision-making within respective roles
  • Cultivates a culture of distributed decision-making
  • Inspires innovation and accountability at all levels

Adoption and Commitment

  • Emphasizes alignment between personal and organizational goals
  • Prioritizes intrinsic and extrinsic motivation
  • Focuses on recognition and reinforcement
  • Ensures continued engagement, real productive usage of the new processes or tools

 

From an SAP perspective, we assembled these factors and change sentiments into a framework aligned with the SAP Activate methodology and focused on SAP systems implementations.

We call this framework Deep Transformation Insights (DTI), utilizing change sentiment questionnaires throughout the implementation cycle for customers. DTI contains assessment tools that can be administered at any stage of the implementation process.

The insights obtained can serve as:

(a) An indicator of the degree of adoption/acceptance among users

(b) An assessment of issues in project enablement that can adversely impact the success of the implementation

(c) A basis for planning and executing learning to enhance user adoption

(d) A regular data driven pulse-check to monitor success of actions to improve the five dimensions.

Customer Case highlighting Activities from Data-Driven Insights to Accelerate User Adoption in SAP S/4HANA Projects

So how could this look like in SAP projects? To make it tangible we would like to share a real world customer case.   

The customer prepared for a planned implementation of SAP S/4HANA supporting an enterprise-wide digital transformation strategy to enhance scalability and efficiency. Further business alignment goals included greater simplicity, efficiency, insight, collaboration, transparency, and compliance, among others.

The company achieved success by prioritizing employee buy-in and minimizing disruption. Using SAP's Deep Transformation Insights service, they enhanced their change management with digital tools and data-driven insights. This people-centric approach effectively managed the transformation's complexity, leading to a successful implementation After implementing the DTI service, the company established and orchestrated an employee engagement feedback architecture using a structured change-sentiment framework across project and organizational units.
Additionally, they gained better insights into stakeholder perceptions and experiences throughout the project lifecycle. They now use these insights as the foundation to accelerate and support solution adoption.

"Utilizing DTI was seamless, and the insights we gained from the team were very thorough, thoughtful, and actionable. We were able to easily see that the work we were doing was having the intended effect because of the results from DTI"
Director Organizational Change Management, Kyndryl

Summary

Organizational Change Management (OCM) is crucial for ensuring successful adoption and realizing the full potential of any digital transformation. However, many organizations face challenges in integrating OCM into their implementation strategy.

Deep Transformation Insights (DTI) offers a comprehensive solution to these challenges, providing a data-driven approach to change management that aligns with the SAP Activate methodology. By focusing on five proven key sentiments—Awareness, Acceptance, Enablement, Empowerment, and Commitment—DTI helps organizations guide their employees through the change process, fostering engagement and long-term success. Find out more about SAP Organizational Change Management on sap.com, or contact us per e-mail.

We look forward to discuss your questions in the comments.

entry.title: SAP S/4HANA 2023 FPS3: What’s in it for the Utilities Industry?
entry.content:
All good things come in threes – therefore please find below (continuing my series of my previous SAP Community blog posts listed at the bottom) an overview of the main innovations dedicated to the utilities industry that are new with SAP S/4HANA FPS3 2023, the third feature pack available for SAP S/4HANA release 2023 since end of February 2025:

  1. Once more the latest feature pack of SAP S/4HANA Utilities offers further modern and flexible analytical capabilities: In continuation of previously delivered roadmap items, you can now extract energy data management (EDM) master data for reporting in SAP Datasphere such as profile headers and profile assignments. For that purpose new CDS views are  available, for details see Energy data management master data for reporting in SAP Datasphere for the utilities industry.
    The second delivered road map item in the area of analytics is Native sales statistics and unbilled revenue reporting in SAP Datasphere and SAP Analytics Cloud. Existing processes and capabilities, such as statistics groups and their assignments, ODP data sources, ODP delta queue, simulation period, and IS-U mass simulation index are being reused. For more technical details please read SAP note 3473213.

  2. Several enhancements are available related to the topics Sales Interface Integration and Utilities Product Integration Layer (UPIL), and mainly targeting retailers in liberalized energy markets:
    • Period control and APIs for life cycle events and contract management processes allows you to define and calculate durations and contract periods using product attributes. These appointments are shown in the utilities sales contract overview as part of S/4HANA Utilities for customer engagement

      Moreover new OData APIs are available to read utilities sales contracts and simulate pricing for utilities. For details see APIs for Utilities Products, Utilities Sales Contracts and Utilities Sales Contract Processes.

    • Streamlining contract migration and bulk data processing offers the ability to migrate relevant parts of the contract history to utilities sales contracts. This history is being shown in utilities sales contract overview, where you can also cancel selected utilities sales contract items in case this is needed after migration.
    • In the context of the Utilities Product Integration Layer (UPIL) and the Sales Integration Interface, it is now possible to use for instance a characteristic for a product-determined price amount to a sales product for a quantity-based price. You can maintain prices, amounts etc. directly on product level. For all details see Extending pricing capabilities.

  3. Once more we have also incorporated enhancements to SAP S/4HANA Utilities based on improvement requests submitted by utility companies from various countries within our SAP Continuous Influence session dedicated to SAP S/4HANA Utilities. For all details see my blog post SAP Continuous Influence Session for SAP S/4HANA Utilities: Status Update January 2025  .

  4. Following the developer extensibility element of SAP's Clean Core strategy, several additional cloud compliant business add-ins (BAdIs) are now available for the most used SAP enhancements (aka user-exits) in IS-U. Please read SAP note 3406389 for a comprehensive overview on Clean Core for SAP S/4HANA Utilities, review the list of related SAP notes.
     
  5. Finally the SAP Fiori app  Business Partner Financial Overview now includes a new facet "IS-U Invoicing Documents" showing utilities-industry specific data



    For details see Display of utilities invoices and budget billing plans in a business partner financial overview .

  6. During the last months, the central SAP for Utilities landing page on SAP Help Portal has been restructured and updated in order to provide a comprehensive overview about SAP S/4HANA Utilities and other available SAP solutions dedicated to the utilities industry. Follow https://help.sap.com/utilities to explore all details.

Finally, as a reference, here ist the list of my previous blog posts related to SAP S/4HANA 2023:

SAP S/4HANA 2023: What’s in it for the Utilities Industry? 
SAP S/4HANA 2023 FPS1: What’s in it for the Utilities Industry? 
SAP S/4HANA 2023 FPS2: What’s in it for the Utilities Industry?

entry.title: Be Part of Tomorrow: Join SAP's Customer Engagement Initiative Cycle 1, 2025!
entry.content:
We’re thrilled to announce the launch of the first cycle of the SAP Customer Engagement Initiative for 2025, featuring 50 exciting new projects aimed at fostering innovation and collaboration with our valued SAP customers and partners.

In today’s fast-paced business world, staying ahead is more important than ever. The SAP Customer Engagement Initiative presents a unique opportunity to gain early insights into SAP’s product developments. By participating, you’ll work directly with our developers and product managers, helping shape the future of our products.

Register until March 28, 2025! Don’t miss out on the opportunity to have your voice heard and influence the next generation of SAP solutions.

To help you explore the available projects, we’ve compiled a detailed list for your convenience. Check out the full list here or follow the links below to discover the exciting opportunities:

ABAP

  • ABAP Cloud Development Assistant
  • OData UI Service from scratch Generator

SAP Business Technology Platform

  • BTP AI based Data Anonymization Service
  • Create AI-driven extensions and automations with SAP Build
  • Improving the Admin's and Solution Architect's Experience on SAP BTP
  • SAP BPM, SAP BRM and SAP Process Orchestration migration to Cloud
  • SAP BTP Cockpit, Cloud Foundry UIs
  • SAP BTP Usability Event - User research project
  • SAP Business Technology Platform: Multi-Region Desaster Recovery
  • SAP Cloud Identity Services - Identity Provisioning, Integration with Joule 
  • SAP Integration Suite

SAP Concur

  • SAP Concur - AI Managed Expense Reports
  • SAP Concur - Dynamic Card Management – Virtual Credit Cards
  • SAP Concur Expense - Web Services for Financial Integration
  • SAP Concur Mobile app and the sign-in user experience
  • SAP Concur Solutions – All About E-Receipts
  • SAP Concur Solutions - Customer Managed Keys for granular control over data
  • SAP Concur Solutions - Sponsored Guest Feature
  • SAP Concur Solutions - Web Notifications Center and Preferences

SAP Customer Experience

  • Research project into a potential loyalty cloud solution
  • SAP CX AI Toolkit

Data & Analytics

  • Redesigning the Dashboard Experience
  • SAP HANA Cloud Elastic Compute Node
  • SAP HANA Database Migration to SAP HANA Cloud

SAP S/4HANA Cloud

  • Bank Relationship Management: Improving Overview, Services & Fee, and Interest Calculations
  • Fiori First for Sales Order Management
  • Identifying and resolving critical situations in a production planners day in S/4HANA Public Cloud
  • Identity and Access Management in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition
  • Insights into Business processes with Business Event Logging
  • Joule and GenAI use cases in SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud
  • New Financial Reporting for Enterprise Project (Project Booklet) including ETO
  • Planned New Solution for Cost Component Breakdown
  • Project staffing, consider dependencies across different roles in a project
  • SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition - Product Change Management
  • Simplification of transports in SAP S/4HANA® Cloud Public Edition, three-system landscape
  • Usability Improvements for managing attachments in S/4HANA business applications

SAP Globalization

  • Australia and New Zealand: GST requirement for Down payments
  • Australia: Modified Payment Times Report - AASB 8 and Taxable Payments Annual Report
  • ISO 21378 Audit Data Collection Standard
  • Planned enhancements to Run statutory reports to handle Draft Report reconciliation
  • Sales and Use Tax Reporting in United States - User Research
  • Sales and Use Tax payments in United States

Lifecycle Management

  • DevOps with ABAP

SAP Integration Experience Feedback goes SAP Customer Engagement Initiative

  • Assisted Assessment of Aligned Business APIs for External Workforce
  • Assisted Assessment of Aligned Business APIs for Hire to Retire Cloud
  • Assisted Assessment of Aligned Business APIs for Source-to-Pay

Others

  • Research into AI-Assisted User Learning Rollout for Adoption
  • SAP Reference Architecture in SAP LeanIX
  • Shape the future of SAP Cloud ALM Configuration & Security Analysis
  • The AI-Driven Future of New Product Introduction (NPI)

We believe that collaboration is key to sparking innovation. By working alongside our customers and partners, we can jointly shape the future of SAP’s products and transform business operations.

Let’s take this journey together. Together, we have the power to drive progress and transform industries.

Don’t miss out – sign up today at influence.sap.com and be a part of the SAP Customer Engagement Initiative Cycle 1 in 2025!

Warm regards, 
Your SAP Customer Engagement Initiative Team 

Follow-up after registration: When you register for a project, you will be invited to an introductory call with the SAP project lead. At this point further participation is optional. Typically, all activities are governed by the Feedback Agreement with SAP.

entry.title: Import Financial Plan Data - Understanding the Plan Data Scope
entry.content:
The Import Financial Plan Data app is available in SAP S/4HANA, SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public edition and SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private edition. It allows key users to import plan data line items from CSV files.

Understanding Plan Data Scope

This blog post will explain how the plan data scope works and how it helps you avoid accidently overwriting data.
Please keep in mind that the Import Financial Plan Data app is only an interface for uploading data into the system. The data has to be maintained and reviewed manually before uploading it, as  only technical checks are performed (for example, checking whether master data exists in the target system) and no business logic is integrated.

Let us explore how to use the Import Financial Plan Data app in a simple scenario for cost center planning.

Example Scenarios

Example 1: Initial Import
In the example provided, you intend to import four plan data records. Since this is the first time the application is being used, no records are present in the database. This means four new records will be incorporated from the CSV file, as shown in the following screens:

After importing the plan data, you then see a success message that allows you to run the Display Financial Plan Data report directly. This report displays the plan data on the corresponding accounts, indicating that the outcome is as expected.

Example 2: Correcting Data
Suppose you notice that one of the amounts in your file was incorrect. To correct this, simply change the amount from 100 to 150 USD, and import the file again. The import process informs you that the existing plan data will be adapted within the plan data scope and that four records will be incorporated from the CSV file.

After the successful import, the Display Financial Plan Data report shows the corrected amount of 150 USD.

Example 3: Adding Missing Accounts
In another example, you may have forgotten to upload the plan data for a specific account. In this case, you merely need to reimport the file, including the new account.

The system notifies you that the existing plan data will be adapted within the plan data scope and that five records will be incorporated from the CSV file.
Consequently, the Display Financial Plan Data report now displays the plan data for the missing account as well.

 

Example 4: Handling Complex Scenarios
Let us now examine how the system handles more complex scenarios or repetitive imports.
To reverse specific records before importing a new CSV file, the system creates a logical query based on the dimensions included in the file. Dimensions marked with an X in row #3 are concatenated with AND, while the different members in this column are concatenated with OR. For dimensions without an X, the members are not specified, so records for all members of that dimension are reversed.

Note:
This may reverse data values for which there is no corresponding row in the source file because the selection defined by the plan data scope reverses all values for which the following is true, for example:
Plan Category = "PLN"
AND General Ledger Fiscal Year = "2025"
AND Posting Period = "011"
AND Company Code = "1710"
AND (Cost Center = "0017101101" OR Cost Center = "17101201")

In example 3, you mistakenly planned on cost center 1710201, which should not have been planned on. The data you imported in this file is incorrect, and now you wish to send only three records instead of five.
To correct this, you reimport the file with these three records, but you leave the X for the cost center dimension in the file.

This action results in the reversal of the records meeting the following criteria:
Plan Category = "PLN"
AND General Ledger Fiscal Year = "2025"
AND Posting Period = "011"
AND Company Code = "1710"
AND Cost Center = "0017101101"

The erroneous cost center data remains unchanged, so the incorrect data for cost center 1710201 is still present in the system after this import.

To correct the erroneous data, you need to remove the X from the cost center column.

This action results in the reversal of the records meeting the following criteria:
Plan Category = "PLN"
AND General Ledger Fiscal Year = "2025"
AND Posting Period = "011"
AND Company Code = "1710"

This sets the records for cost centers not specified in the CSV file to zero.

 

Example 5: Updating and Adding Records
In this last example, you want to correct one existing record for 2025 from 150 USD to 200 USD and add new records for 2026.

Let’s assume that the data in your system looks as follows:

By importing the CSV file as shown above, the system reverses the plan records for both cost centers and both years. Note, however, that even records that were not part of the uploaded CSV file are also reversed because of the following criteria:
Plan Category = "PLN"
AND (General Ledger Fiscal Year = "2025" OR General Ledger Fiscal Year = "2026")
AND Posting Period = "011"
AND Company Code = "1710"
AND (Cost Center = "0017101101" OR Cost Center ="0017101201")

To resolve this, you need to split your data into two upload files.

One CSV file contains the record that needs to be corrected and where the X is set for all dimensions, including the G/L account.

This changes the amount from 150 USD to 200 USD, while all other records remain unchanged.

The second file contains the new records for 2026, where you don’t need to set the X for the G/L account dimension.

After both CSV files have been uploaded, the data in your system looks as follows:

Conclusion:
To prevent data from being overwritten unintentionally, uploaded data must be disjointed. A clear understanding of the plan data scope ensures that records can be added, updated, and reversed safely.

entry.title: Benefit From SAP Master Data Governance on SAP S/4HANA 2023 Feature Pack Stack 03
entry.content:
To complete the blog series about innovations in SAP Master Data Governance (MDG) coming in SAP S/4HANA 2023 (Initial Stack I Feature Pack Stack 01 I Feature Pack Stack 02), I'd now like to share delta information about the key MDG features coming with Feature Pack Stack 03.

Enabling field extensibility in customer and supplier views (cloud-ready mode)

To increase the effectiveness in master data governance of business-partner data in cloud-ready mode, we have enabled the following field extensibility capabilities: 

  • Apply field extensibility (custom fields) not only in the business-partner core view (BUT000), but also in customer and supplier views.
  • Extend fields in the following views:
    • Customer core view (KNA1)
    • Customer company code view (KNB1)
    • Customer sales area core view (KNVV)
    • Supplier core view (LFA1)
    • Supplier company code view (LFB1)
    • Supplier purchasing org view (LFM1)

Field extensibility in supplier view (cloud-ready mode)

Assignment of data controllers to a business partner in the classic mode of central governance

It is now possible to manually assign one or more data controllers to a business partner and set the "Invalid Assignment Status" for existing data controllers. Additionally, the system can immediately derive data controllers when the corresponding organizational units are maintained.

Setting the "Invalid Assignment Status" for existing data controllers

Enhanced validation of changes in financial data 

To avoid errors in the governance of financial data, checks for the most business-critical attributes can be made with remote function calls to target systems to make sure that proposed changes in MDG are validated before change request activation.

Defining target systems for remote validation and display of validation results in MDG processing

Gen AI-based assisted change for custom objects and summary of mass changes*

To further increase the effectiveness through AI in central governance​, we have enabled AI-assisted changes for reuse entities in custom objects and the AI-based summary of change requests with multiple objects (mass changes).

AI-assisted change and AI-based summary of changes

(* Please note that these Gen AI-based features are only available with SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud Edition)

See also the presentation outlining the MDG delta content coming with SAP S/4HANA 2023 FPS03.

To get the full picture of planned innovations in SAP Master Data Governance across the entire MDG solution portfolio, see the SAP Roadmap Explorer.

To stay up to date, simply follow the SAP Community Topic Page for SAP Master Data Governance and join the Q&A and discussion forum.

entry.title: What is SAP RISE ? Technical Architect Perspective to ABAP, migrations-options, tools
entry.content:
RISE with SAP is a comprehensive, business-transformation-as-a-service offering from SAP designed to help organizations modernize their enterprise systems, migrate to the cloud, and adopt SAP S/4HANA as their core ERP platform. Launched in early 2021, it’s a bundled solution that combines software, services, and infrastructure into a single subscription-based contract, aimed at simplifying the journey to an intelligent enterprise. It’s particularly relevant for companies transitioning from legacy SAP systems like ECC to the cloud-native, HANA-powered S/4HANA, while leveraging advanced technologies like AI, analytics, and process automation.

Core Concept of RISE with SAP

RISE with SAP is not just a product but a holistic program that shifts the traditional on-premise ERP model to a cloud-centric, managed-service approach. It’s built around three key pillars:

 

  1. Move to SAP S/4HANA: Encourages adoption of S/4HANA as the digital core, replacing older systems like ECC, with a focus on cloud deployment (public, private, or hybrid).
  2. Cloud Transformation: Provides the infrastructure and tools to operate in a cloud environment, reducing the burden of managing hardware and software stacks.
  3. Intelligent Enterprise: Integrates advanced capabilities (e.g., AI, machine learning, IoT) to streamline processes and drive innovation.

The “RISE” acronym reflects its promise: Realize value, Innovate rapidly, Simplify operations, and Evolve continuously. (** this is not official acronym from SAP, it is derived from what its DNA) 

Key Components of RISE with SAP

RISE with SAP bundles several elements into one offering, managed under a single SLA (Service Level Agreement) with SAP or its partners. Here’s what’s included:

SAP S/4HANA Cloud: The ERP software itself, available in:

  1. Public Cloud: Fully managed by SAP, multi-tenant, with standardized processes.
  2. Private Cloud: Single-tenant, customizable, hosted on hyperscalers like AWS, Azure, or Google Cloud.
  3. Hybrid: Combines on-premise and cloud elements. Runs on SAP HANA, enabling real-time data processing and simplified data models.

RISE Tools and Services:

  1. SAP Business Process Intelligence (BPI): Analyzes and optimizes processes before migration (e.g., using tools like Signavio).
  2. SAP Readiness Check: Assesses legacy systems for S/4HANA compatibility, flagging custom code and simplification items.
  3. SAP Migration Cockpit: Automates data migration from ECC to S/4HANA.
  4. SAP Activate Methodology: A structured approach (Prepare, Explore, Realize, Deploy) for project execution.
  5. Custom Code Migration App: Helps adapt ABAP code for S/4HANA and cloud.
  6. SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP): A cloud platform for extensions, integrations, and innovations (e.g., building apps with ABAP RAP, hosting AI models). Supports the “clean core” principle—keeping S/4HANA standard while offloading customizations to BTP.
  7. Embedded Analytics and Intelligence: Tools like SAP Analytics Cloud and embedded AI capabilities (e.g., predictive analytics in S/4HANA) for real-time insights.
  8. Managed Services: SAP handles upgrades, patches, and maintenance, shifting responsibility from the customer to SAP and its ecosystem partners.

How RISE with SAP Works in Practice

Imagine a company running ECC on-premise with custom ABAP code, complex integrations, and aging hardware. RISE with SAP offers a streamlined path:

  • Assessment: Use Readiness Check to analyze ECC—say, identifying 500 custom programs needing rework.
  • Strategy: Choose a migration approach (Greenfield, Brownfield, or Selective Data Transition) and a cloud model (e.g., Azure Private Cloud).
  • Execution: Leverage Migration Cockpit for data, BTP for custom apps, and SAP Activate for project management.
  • Operation: SAP manages the Azure-hosted S/4HANA instance, applying quarterly updates and ensuring 99.9% uptime.

The subscription covers licensing, infrastructure, and services, with costs based on factors like user count, system size, and cloud provider.

ABAP and Technical Perspective

For an SAP Technical Architect with ABAP expertise, RISE with SAP shifts the focus:

  1. Code Modernization: Legacy ABAP (e.g., BDCs, obsolete FMs must be rewritten for S/4HANA (e.g., CDS views, AMDP, RAP). The Custom Code Migration App flags issues, but architects design solutions—like moving a Z-report to a HANA-optimized CDS view exposed via OData.
  2. Clean Core: Customizations move to BTP, requiring ABAP developers to master RAP (RESTful ABAP Programming) or CAP (Cloud Application Programming) instead of modifying S/4HANA directly.
